Data Collection¶

Data was downloaded from BigQuery's publicly available dataset. Kaggle was used to extract the data, as they have a free integration with BigQuery. My script for downloading the data is here.

We ended up having 2 datasets:

  • Attributes related to trips per a given time point.
  • Geometric data.

We'll be building a regression model to predict fare_amount. We'll also look at some interesting properties/patterns that has something to do with the geometric data.

The first few rows of these datasets are shown below.

Data Cleaning¶

  • Incorrect dates.
    • We'll limit the dataset for the year 2018 only.
  • Max fare_amount is ~$4,300.
    • We'll only consider fare amounts that are less than the 99th percentile.
minimum date 2008-12-31 00:00:00
maxmium date 2081-06-24 00:00:00
Out[7]:
rate_code passenger_count trip_distance fare_amount tip_amount payment_type pickup_id dropoff_id
count 483005.000000 483005.000000 483005.000000 483005.000000 483005.000000 483005.000000 483005.000000 483005.000000
mean 1.462467 1.333489 10.384775 36.575097 1.976814 1.204971 133.645703 119.145344
std 1.402275 0.995965 5.327600 18.753280 3.731926 0.415559 74.992084 76.406088

50% 1.000000 1.000000 9.300000 32.500000 0.000000 1.000000 133.000000 95.000000

fare_amount 99th percentile 84.0

Let's look at the distribution of fare_amount after data cleaning. Now the data makes more sense. We have a lower bound at zero, and we observe a distribution that's close to an exponential distribution from ~25 onwards.

Next, we will join the trip attributes with the geometric data. Final dataset is shown below.

We can observe a linear relationship between tip_amount and trip_distance. For this analysis, we refrain from performing complicated feature engineering, as simpler analysis tend to generalize better.

Geometric Properties¶

Now we will plot the average of the trip attributes together with the geometric data. To summarize the charts below, it is clear that there are some interesting patterns that can be observed. (i.e. It shows that some zone ID's have a relatively higher average fare_amount)

Note: The process of plotting the geometric data and trip attributes can be made interactive. However, since we're not allowed to share this notebook on a public repository, i can't use Viola to serve an interactive jupyter notebook.

Different Boroughs in New York City¶

Average Fare Amount for New York City¶

  • fare_amount is highest in Staten Island

Average Trip Distance for New York City¶

Average Tip Amount for New York City¶

  • It makes sense that tip_amount is also highest in Staten Island, since tips and fare are correlated.

Machine Learning¶

First, we fit a linear regression model. We obtained a satisfactory performance. Our RMSE (both on Cross Validation and Testing) is just less than $2. This performance is great considering the distribution of the fare_amount.

SKLEARN FIT (Splitting the dataset to train and testing)¶

Model RMSE with 3 cross validation : 1.8479621163826945
Model Test RMSE 1.8401913328109212
Average Salary in the Dataset 37.02352364678449

Fitting the entire dataset with R. (For better interpretability)¶

  • Coefficients are statistically significant, except for zone_id. As expected, tip_amount has the highest positive coefficient.
Call:
lm(formula = fare_amount ~ tip_amount + trip_distance + zone_id,
    data = data)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max
-5.9955 -0.9052 -0.0922  0.9730  9.6500

Coefficients:
               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)   10.900811   0.545341  19.989  < 2e-16 ***
tip_amount     2.368028   0.043397  54.566  < 2e-16 ***
trip_distance  0.362769   0.090533   4.007 8.07e-05 ***
zone_id       -0.001075   0.001389  -0.774     0.44
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 1.692 on 255 degrees of freedom
